expanded_description: Combining multiple conversion factors to convert between mass, moles, and particle count in a single calculation sequence.

core_concept.principle: Multi-step conversions chain together conversion factors (molar mass and Avogadro's constant) to convert between any two quantities: mass, moles, or particles.
core_concept.explanation: Rather than performing separate calculations for each conversion step, multi-step conversions combine all factors into a single dimensional analysis setup. This reduces rounding errors and provides a clear path from given information to desired answer.

image.alt: Diagram showing conversion pathways between mass, moles, and particles
image.caption: All conversions pass through moles as the central hub

sections.introduction: In multi-step conversions, moles serve as the central hub. You cannot convert directly between mass and particles—you must pass through moles first. This creates a clear pathway: mass ↔ moles ↔ particles.

key_concept.definition: The mole is the bridge quantity: all conversions between mass and particles require passing through moles using molar mass and Avogadro's constant.
key_concept.context: This pathway ensures dimensional consistency and prevents calculation errors.

principles.statement: Moles are the central conversion point; mass and particles cannot be directly converted without passing through moles.
principles.application: Always identify whether you need to convert to moles first, then to the final quantity.
principles.example: To convert grams to atoms: $\text{g} \xrightarrow{\text{molar mass}} \text{mol} \xrightarrow{N_A} \text{atoms}$
principles.analogy: Like changing currencies through a common base currency—you can't convert directly from yen to euros without going through dollars first.

components.definition: Multi-step conversions require two conversion factors: molar mass (g/mol) and Avogadro's constant (particles/mol).
components.interpretation: The mole units cancel in the middle, leaving the desired final unit.

image.alt: Triangle showing mass, moles, and particles at each corner
image.caption: The conversion triangle shows all possible pathways through moles

worked_examples.title: Converting Mass to Atoms
worked_examples.problem: How many atoms of aluminum are in $10.0$ grams of aluminum?

step_by_step.explanation: Mass (g) → Moles (mol) → Atoms (particles)
From periodic table: Al = $26.98$ g/mol
Chain the conversion factors so units cancel properly
step_by_step.calculation: $? \text{ atoms} = 10.0 \text{ g Al} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ mol Al}}{26.98 \text{ g Al}} \times \dfrac{6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ atoms}}{1 \text{ mol Al}}$

step_by_step.explanation: Multiply across numerator, divide by denominator; round to 3 significant figures

worked_examples.concept_applied: Mass-to-atoms conversion requires two steps: mass → moles → atoms.

sections.key_concept: The reverse pathway converts from particles to mass: particles → moles → mass, using the inverse of each conversion factor.

classifications.value: Used when counting individual atoms and needing measurable mass
classifications.behavior: Common in nanotechnology and atomic-scale calculations

classifications.value: Used for molecular compounds when particle count is known
classifications.behavior: Common in gas calculations and molecular biology

classifications.value: Used for ionic compounds when formula unit count is known

worked_examples.title: Converting Atoms to Mass
worked_examples.problem: What is the mass in grams of $1.50 \times 10^{24}$ atoms of copper?

step_by_step.explanation: Atoms → Moles → Mass (g)
From periodic table: Cu = $63.55$ g/mol
Divide by $N_A$ to get moles, then multiply by molar mass
step_by_step.calculation: $? \text{ g} = 1.50 \times 10^{24} \text{ atoms} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ mol}}{6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ atoms}} \times \dfrac{63.55 \text{ g}}{1 \text{ mol}}$

step_by_step.explanation: Divide first, then multiply; round to 3 significant figures

worked_examples.concept_applied: Particles-to-mass conversion reverses the pathway: atoms → moles → mass.

worked_examples.title: Converting Molecules to Mass for a Compound
worked_examples.problem: Calculate the mass of $2.50 \times 10^{22}$ molecules of carbon dioxide ($\text{CO}_2$).

step_by_step.explanation: Molecules → Moles → Grams
step_by_step.calculation: $? \text{ g} = 2.50 \times 10^{22} \text{ molecules} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ mol}}{6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ molecules}} \times \dfrac{44.01 \text{ g}}{1 \text{ mol}}$

step_by_step.explanation: Divide by $N_A$, then multiply by molar mass

worked_examples.concept_applied: Compound conversions require calculating molar mass first, then using the same pathway.

sections.section_title: Converting Between Particles of Different Elements in a Compound
sections.introduction: When working with compounds, you may need to convert between particles of the compound and particles of individual elements within it. This requires using the chemical formula subscripts as additional conversion factors.

key_concept.definition: Chemical formula subscripts provide mole ratios between compound and constituent elements.
key_concept.context: For example, $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ contains 2 H atoms per molecule, so $1$ mol $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ contains $2$ mol H atoms.

principles.statement: The subscript in a chemical formula indicates the mole ratio between the compound and each element.
principles.application: Use subscripts as conversion factors: $\dfrac{\text{mol element}}{\text{mol compound}}$.
principles.analogy: Like knowing a dozen eggs comes in packages of 12—you use the package ratio to convert between packages and individual eggs.

worked_examples.title: Finding Atoms of an Element from Compound Mass
worked_examples.problem: How many hydrogen atoms are in $5.00$ grams of water ($\text{H}_2\text{O}$)?

step_by_step.explanation: Mass $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ → Moles $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ → Moles H → Atoms H

step_by_step.step: Set up dimensional analysis with all factors
step_by_step.explanation: Include molar mass, subscript ratio, and Avogadro's constant
step_by_step.calculation: $? \text{ H atoms} = 5.00 \text{ g } \text{H}_2\text{O} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ mol } \text{H}_2\text{O}}{18.02 \text{ g } \text{H}_2\text{O}} \times \dfrac{2 \text{ mol H}}{1 \text{ mol } \text{H}_2\text{O}} \times \dfrac{6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ H atoms}}{1 \text{ mol H}}$

step_by_step.explanation: Multiply all numerators, divide by all denominators; round to 3 significant figures

worked_examples.concept_applied: Formula subscripts add an extra conversion step between compound moles and element moles.

worked_examples.title: Finding Mass of an Element from Compound Particles
worked_examples.problem: What is the mass of oxygen in $1.20 \times 10^{24}$ molecules of glucose ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6$)?

step_by_step.explanation: Molecules glucose → Moles glucose → Moles O → Mass O
Each glucose molecule contains 6 O atoms, so $\dfrac{6 \text{ mol O}}{1 \text{ mol glucose}}$
Chain all conversion factors with proper unit cancellation
step_by_step.calculation: $? \text{ g O} = 1.20 \times 10^{24} \text{ molecules} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ mol glucose}}{6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ molecules}} \times \dfrac{6 \text{ mol O}}{1 \text{ mol glucose}} \times \dfrac{16.00 \text{ g O}}{1 \text{ mol O}}$

step_by_step.explanation: Multiply across and divide; round to 3 significant figures

worked_examples.concept_applied: Multi-step conversions can extract mass of individual elements from compound particle counts.

sections.key_concept: Real-world chemistry problems often require combining multiple conversion types in a single calculation sequence.

classifications.value: Convert mass of compound to atoms of a specific element within it
Convert particle count of compound to mass of a specific element
classifications.behavior: Three conversion factors in reverse order

classifications.value: Determine which sample contains more particles or mass

examples.process: Which has more atoms: 10 g Fe or 10 g Al?
examples.explanation: Convert both to atoms, then compare numerical values

worked_examples.title: Comparing Atom Counts in Equal Masses
worked_examples.problem: Which contains more atoms: $10.0$ g of iron (Fe) or $10.0$ g of aluminum (Al)?

step_by_step.explanation: Fe: $M = 55.85$ g/mol
step_by_step.calculation: $N_{\text{Fe}} = 10.0 \text{ g} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ mol}}{55.85 \text{ g}} \times \dfrac{6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ atoms}}{1 \text{ mol}} = 1.08 \times 10^{23} \text{ atoms}$

step_by_step.explanation: Al: $M = 26.98$ g/mol
step_by_step.calculation: $N_{\text{Al}} = 10.0 \text{ g} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ mol}}{26.98 \text{ g}} \times \dfrac{6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ atoms}}{1 \text{ mol}} = 2.23 \times 10^{23} \text{ atoms}$

step_by_step.explanation: Lower molar mass means more atoms per gram

worked_examples.final_answer: $10.0$ g of aluminum contains more atoms ($2.23 \times 10^{23}$ vs. $1.08 \times 10^{23}$).
worked_examples.concept_applied: Equal masses of different elements contain different numbers of atoms based on molar mass.

worked_examples.title: Total Atoms in a Compound Sample
worked_examples.problem: How many total atoms are in $3.00$ grams of ammonia ($\text{NH}_3$)?

step_by_step.explanation: Convert mass to molecules first
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{Molecules} = 3.00 \text{ g} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ mol}}{17.03 \text{ g}} \times \dfrac{6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ molecules}}{1 \text{ mol}} = 1.061 \times 10^{23} \text{ molecules}$

step_by_step.explanation: Each $\text{NH}_3$ has 1 N + 3 H = 4 total atoms
Multiply molecules by atoms per molecule

worked_examples.concept_applied: Total atom count requires multiplying molecule count by atoms per molecule.

sections.key_concept: Multi-step conversions are prone to errors. Systematic approaches help ensure accuracy.

classifications.value: Attempting to convert directly between mass and particles
classifications.behavior: Produces answers off by factors of $N_A$ or molar mass

examples.explanation: Must go through moles: g → mol → atoms

classifications.value: Forgetting to use formula subscripts when counting element atoms
classifications.behavior: Answers off by factors equal to subscripts

examples.explanation: Must multiply by 2 for H atoms per water molecule

classifications.value: Not labeling units clearly throughout calculation
classifications.behavior: Leads to mixing up conversion factors

examples.explanation: Should specify: mol $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ vs mol H atoms

worked_examples.title: Problem-Solving Strategy: Unit Map
worked_examples.problem: Develop a unit map for converting $25.0$ g of $\text{CaCO}_3$ to oxygen atoms.

step_by_step.explanation: Start: g $\text{CaCO}_3$; End: O atoms
g $\text{CaCO}_3$ → mol $\text{CaCO}_3$ → mol O → O atoms
Molar mass of $\text{CaCO}_3$, subscript (3 O per $\text{CaCO}_3$), $N_A$
Chain all factors with proper unit cancellation
step_by_step.calculation: $? = 25.0 \text{ g} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ mol}}{100.09 \text{ g}} \times \dfrac{3 \text{ mol O}}{1 \text{ mol}} \times \dfrac{6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ atoms}}{1 \text{ mol}} = 4.51 \times 10^{23} \text{ O atoms}$

worked_examples.final_answer: $4.51 \times 10^{23}$ oxygen atoms; Strategy: always map units before calculating.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Unit mapping prevents errors by visualizing the conversion pathway before calculation.

summary: Multi-step conversions combine molar mass and Avogadro's constant to convert between mass, moles, and particles in a single calculation. The mole serves as the central hub—all conversions between mass and particles must pass through moles. For compounds, chemical formula subscripts provide additional conversion factors between compound moles and element moles. Always use dimensional analysis to track units and verify that unwanted units cancel. Common errors include skipping the mole step, forgetting subscripts, and poor unit tracking. The systematic approach: (1) identify starting and ending units, (2) map the conversion pathway, (3) gather all conversion factors, (4) set up dimensional analysis with unit cancellation, (5) calculate and apply significant figures. Mastery of multi-step conversions is essential for stoichiometry, empirical formula determination, and all advanced quantitative chemistry.
